## 8. A review of open source ventilators for COVID-19 and future pandemics
Valid Text Names:
'pearce2020areviewof pages 1-3', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 3-4', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 4-5', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 5-6', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 6-7', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 7-8', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 8-9', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 9-10', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 10-11', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 11-12', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 12-13', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 13-14', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 13-13', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 14-16', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 16-18', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 18-20', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 20-22', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 22-24', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 24-27', 'pearce2020areviewof pages 27-27'

BibTex:
@article{pearce2020areviewof,
    author = "Pearce, Joshua M.",
    title = "A review of open source ventilators for COVID-19 and future pandemics",
    year = "2020",
    journal = "F1000Research",
    volume = "9",
    month = "Mar",
    doi = "10.12688/f1000research.22942.2",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.12688/f1000research.22942.2",
    pages = "218",
    publisher = "F1000 Research Ltd",
    issn = "2046-1402"
}


Abstract:
Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) threatens to overwhelm our medical infrastructure at the regional level causing spikes in mortality rates because of shortages of critical equipment, like ventilators. Fortunately, with the recent development and widespread deployment of small-scale manufacturing technologies like RepRap-class 3-D printers and open source microcontrollers, mass distributed manufacturing of ventilators has the potential to overcome medical supply shortages. In this study, after providing a background on ventilators, the academic literature is reviewed to find the existing and already openly-published, vetted designs for ventilators systems. These articles are analyzed to determine if the designs are open source both in spirit (license) as well as practical details (e.g. possessing accessible design source files, bill of materials, assembly instructions, wiring diagrams, firmware and software as well as operation and calibration instructions). Next, the existing Internet and gray literature are reviewed for open source ventilator projects and designs. The results of this review found that the tested and peer-reviewed systems lacked complete documentation and the open systems that were documented were either at the very early stages of design (sometimes without even a prototype) and were essentially only basically tested (if at all). With the considerably larger motivation of an ongoing pandemic, it is assumed these projects will garner greater attention and resources to make significant progress to reach a functional and easily-replicated system. There is a large amount of future work needed to move open source ventilators up to the level considered scientific-grade equipment, and even further work needed to reach medical-grade hardware. Future work is needed to achieve the potential of this approach by developing policies, updating regulations, and securing funding mechanisms for the development and testing of open source ventilators for both the current COVID19 pandemic as well as for future pandemics and for everyday use in low-resource settings.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Quality assessment in industrial applications is often carried out through visual inspection, usually performed or supported by human domain experts. However, the manual visual inspection of processes and products is error-prone and expensive. It is therefore not surprising that the automation of visual inspection in manufacturing and maintenance is heavily researched and discussed. The use of artificial intelligence as an approach to visual inspection in industrial applications has been considered for decades. Recent successes, driven by advances in deep learning, present a possible paradigm shift and have the potential to facilitate automated visual inspection, even under complex environmental conditions. For this reason, we explore the question of to what extent deep learning is already being used in the field of automated visual inspection and which potential improvements to the state of the art could be realized utilizing concepts from academic research. By conducting an extensive review of the openly accessible literature, we provide an overview of proposed and in-use deep-learning models presented in recent years. Our survey consists of 196 open-access publications, of which 31.7% are manufacturing use cases and 68.3% are maintenance use cases. Furthermore, the survey also shows that the majority of the models currently in use are based on convolutional neural networks, the current de facto standard for image classification, object recognition, or object segmentation tasks. Nevertheless, we see the emergence of vision transformer models that seem to outperform convolutional neural networks but require more resources, which also opens up new research opportunities for the future. Another finding is that in 97% of the publications, the authors use supervised learning techniques to train their models. However, with the median dataset size consisting of 2500 samples, deep-learning models cannot be trained from scratch, so it would be beneficial to use other training paradigms, such as self-supervised learning. In addition, we identified a gap of approximately three years between approaches from deep-learning-based computer vision being published and their introduction in industrial visual inspection applications. Based on our findings, we additionally discuss potential future developments in the area of automated visual inspection.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Cloud services are critical to society. However, their reliability is poorly understood. Towards solving the problem, we propose a standard repository for cloud uptime data. We populate this repository with the data we collect containing failure reports from users and operators of cloud services, web services, and online games. The multiple vantage points help reduce bias from individual users and operators. We compare our new data to existing failure data from the Failure Trace Archive and the Google cluster trace. We analyze the MTBF and MTTR, time patterns, failure severity, user-reported symptoms, and operator-reported symptoms of failures in the data we collect. We observe that high-level user facing services fail less often than low-level infrastructure services, likely due to them using fault-tolerance techniques. We use simulation-based experiments to demonstrate the impact of different failure traces on the performance of checkpointing and retry mechanisms. We release the data, and the analysis and simulation tools, as open-source artifacts available at https://github.com/atlarge-research/cloud-uptime-archive .

Abstract:
Open Science is a phenomenon that can be traced back to the Middle Ages. In the end of the twentieth century and the beginning of the twenty-first century, Open Science is strongly growing due to the worldwide internet and related new technologies, tools and communication channels. Two core objectives (reliability and trust) and three main characteristics (transparency, openness and reproducibility) of Open Science can be identified but it is still too early for a broad definition of this growing movement. Its growth is happening in many disciplines and in diverse facets. This article presents an overview of how Open Science is introduced and established in all three science dimensions of research design, processes and publications. For the future, the benefits are analysed that Open Science is offering, as well as the challenges that it is facing. It can be concluded that it is desirable that all researchers collaborate in Open Science. Open Science can improve the different science disciplines, research practices and science in general. In that way, Open Science can contribute to overcome the post-truth age through increasing objective and subjective credibility of science and research. And in the long-term perspective, Open Science can improve the whole research, education, as well as our society.

Abstract:
Advances in wearable sensing and mobile computing have enabled the collection of health and well-being data outside of traditional laboratory and hospital settings, paving the way for a new era of mobile health. Meanwhile, artificial intelligence (AI) has made significant strides in various domains, demonstrating its potential to revolutionize healthcare. Devices can now diagnose diseases, predict heart irregularities and unlock the full potential of human cognition. However, the application of machine learning (ML) to mobile health sensing poses unique challenges due to noisy sensor measurements, high-dimensional data, sparse and irregular time series, heterogeneity in data, privacy concerns and resource constraints. Despite the recognition of the value of mobile sensing, leveraging these datasets has lagged behind other areas of ML. Furthermore, obtaining quality annotations and ground truth for such data is often expensive or impractical. While recent large-scale longitudinal studies have shown promise in leveraging wearable sensor data for health monitoring and prediction, they also introduce new challenges for data modelling. This paper explores the challenges and opportunities of human-centred AI for mobile health, focusing on key sensing modalities such as audio, location and activity tracking. We discuss the limitations of current approaches and propose potential solutions.

Abstract:
Conversations about open science have reached the mainstream, yet many open science practices such as data sharing remain uncommon. Our efforts towards openness therefore need to increase in scale and aim for a more ambitious target. We need an ecosystem not only where research outputs are openly shared but also in which transparency permeates the research process from the start and lends itself to more rigorous and collaborative research. To support this vision, this Essay provides an overview of a selection of open science initiatives from the past 2 decades, focusing on methods transparency, scholarly communication, team science, and research culture, and speculates about what the future of open science could look like. It then draws on these examples to provide recommendations for how funders, institutions, journals, regulators, and other stakeholders can create an environment that is ripe for improvement.

    year = "2023",
    journal = "Proceedings of the 50th Annual International Symposium on Computer Architecture",
    pages = "1-16",

    doi = "10.1145/3579371.3589049",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1145/3579371.3589049",
    publisher = "ACM"

Abstract:
Machine learning (ML) has become a prevalent approach to tame the complexity of design space exploration for domain-specific architectures. While appealing, using ML for design space exploration poses several challenges. First, it is not straightforward to identify the most suitable algorithm from an ever-increasing pool of ML methods. Second, assessing the trade-offs between performance and sample efficiency across these methods is inconclusive. Finally, the lack of a holistic framework for fair, reproducible, and objective comparison across these methods hinders the progress of adopting ML-aided architecture design space exploration and impedes creating repeatable artifacts. To mitigate these challenges, we introduce ArchGym, an open-source gymnasium and easy-to-extend framework that connects a diverse range of search algorithms to architecture simulators. To demonstrate its utility, we evaluate ArchGym across multiple vanilla and domain-specific search algorithms in the design of a custom memory controller, deep neural network accelerators, and a custom SoC for AR/VR workloads, collectively encompassing over 21K experiments. The results suggest that with an unlimited number of samples, ML algorithms are equally favorable to meet the user-defined target specification if its hyperparameters are tuned thoroughly; no one solution is necessarily better than another (e.g., reinforcement learning vs. Bayesian methods). We coin the term "hyperparameter lottery" to describe the relatively probable chance for a search algorithm to find an optimal design provided meticulously selected hyperparameters. Additionally, the ease of data collection and aggregation in ArchGym facilitates research in ML-aided architecture design space exploration. As a case study, we show this advantage by developing a proxy cost model with an RMSE of 0.61% that offers a 2,000-fold reduction in simulation time. Code and data for ArchGym is available at https://bit.ly/ArchGym.

The excerpt describes GOSH "gatherings" as the movement’s core global meetings used to deliberate, learn, make, and set collective values and strategies. Early gatherings produced the manifesto (2016), roadmap (2017) and action plan (2018). The events use an open, collaborative/unconference-style program: about 70% of the program is defined on demand during the meeting and combines hands-on workshops, building and using OSH, discussion sessions chosen by the audience, and group deliberation. Organizers intentionally deploy mechanisms to ensure participant diversity so the community can represent many perspectives and tailor messages to different audiences. Attendance is free (with a suggested contributory fee to subsidize others); funding comes from grants, crowdfunding and sometimes personal/organizer support to enable participation. The gatherings are volunteer-run, produce documents published online for wider collaboration and legitimacy, and are amplified through global community channels. A world distribution of GOSH initiatives is noted (Figure 2), but specific dates and locations of individual gatherings are not listed in this excerpt.

Abstract:
From accelerating simulations and exploring chemical space, to experimental planning and integrating automation within experimental labs, artificial intelligence (AI) is changing the landscape of chemistry. We are seeing a significant increase in the number of publications leveraging these powerful data-driven insights and models to accelerate all aspects of chemical research. For example, how we represent molecules and materials to computer algorithms for predictive and generative models, as well as the physical mechanisms by which we perform experiments in the lab for automation. Here, we present ten diverse perspectives on the impact of AI coming from those with a range of backgrounds from experimental chemistry, computational chemistry, computer science, engineering and across different areas of chemistry, including drug discovery, catalysis, chemical automation, chemical physics, materials chemistry. The ten perspectives presented here cover a range of themes, including AI for computation, facilitating discovery, supporting experiments, and enabling technologies for transformation. We highlight and discuss imminent challenges and ways in which we are redefining problems to accelerate the impact of chemical research via AI.

Abstract:
Recent advancements in artificial intelligence and automation are transforming catalyst discovery and design from traditional trial-and-error manual mode into intelligent, high-throughput digital methodologies. This transformation is driven by four key components, including high-throughput information extraction, automated robotic experimentation, real-time feedback for iterative optimization, and interpretable machine learning for generating new knowledge. These innovations have given rise to the development of self-driving labs and significantly accelerated materials research. Over the past two years, the emergence of large language models (LLMs) has added a new dimension to this field, providing unprecedented flexibility in information integration, decision-making, and interacting with human researchers. This review explores how LLMs are reshaping catalyst design, heralding a revolutionary change in the fields.

Abstract:
Automated synthesis planning has recently re-emerged as a research area at the intersection of chemistry and machine learning. Despite the appearance of steady progress, we argue that imperfect benchmarks and inconsistent comparisons mask systematic shortcomings of existing techniques, and unnecessarily hamper progress. To remedy this, we present a synthesis planning library with an extensive benchmarking framework, called SYNTHESEUS, which promotes best practice by default, enabling consistent meaningful evaluation of single-step and multi-step synthesis planning algorithms. We demonstrate the capabilities of SYNTHESEUS by re-evaluating several previous retrosynthesis algorithms, and find that the ranking of state-of-the-art models changes in controlled evaluation experiments. We end with guidance for future works in this area, and call on the community to engage in the discussion on how to improve benchmarks for synthesis planning.

Abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>High‐throughput experimentation (HTE), the miniaturization and parallelization of reactions, is a valuable tool for accelerating diverse compound library generation, optimizing reaction conditions, and enabling data collection for machine learning (ML) applications. When applied to organic synthesis and methodology, HTE still poses various challenges due to the diverse workflows and reagents required, motivating advancements in reaction design, execution, analysis, and data management. To address these limitations, cutting‐edge technologies, automation, and artificial intelligence (AI) have been implemented to standardize protocols, enhance reproducibility, and improve efficiency. Additionally, strategies to reduce bias and promote serendipitous discoveries have further strengthened HTE's impact. This review highlights recent advances at every stage of the HTE workflow, including the development of customized workflows, diverse analysis, and improved data management practices for greater accessibility and shareability. Furthermore, we examine the current state of the field, outstanding challenges, and future directions toward transforming HTE into a fully integrated, flexible, and democratized platform that drives innovation in organic synthesis.</jats:p>

Abstract:
Materials discovery lays the foundation for many technological advancements. The prediction and discovery of new materials are not simple tasks. Here, we outline some basic principles of solid-state chemistry, which might help to advance both, and discuss pitfalls and challenges in materials discovery. Using the recent work of Szymanski et al. [Nature 624, 86 (2023)], which reported the autonomous discovery of 43 novel materials, as an example, we discuss problems that can arise in unsupervised materials discovery and hope that by addressing these, autonomous materials discovery can be brought closer to reality. We discuss all 43 synthetic products and point out four common shortfalls in the analysis. These errors unfortunately lead to the conclusion that no new materials have been discovered in that work. We conclude that there are two important points of improvement that require future work from the community, as follows. (i) Automated Rietveld analysis of powder x-ray diﬀraction data is not yet reliable. Future improvement of such, and the development of a reliable artiﬁcial-intelligence-based tool for Rietveld ﬁt-ting, would be very helpful, not only for autonomous materials discovery but also for the community in general. (ii) We ﬁnd that disorder in materials is often neglected in predictions. The predicted compounds investigated herein have all their elemental components located on distinct crystallographic positions but in reality, elements can share crystallographic sites, resulting in higher-symmetry space groups and—very often—known alloys or solid solutions. This error might be related to the diﬃculty of modeling disorder in a computationally economical way and needs to be addressed both by computational and experimental material scientists. We ﬁnd that two thirds of the claimed successful materials in Szymanski et al. are likely to be known compositionally disordered versions of the predicted ordered compounds. We highlight important issues in materials discovery, computational chemistry, and autonomous interpretation of x-ray diﬀraction. We discuss concepts of materials discovery from an experimentalist point of view, which we hope will be helpful for the community to further advance this important new aspect of our ﬁeld.

Abstract:
Accelerating R&D is essential to address some of the challenges humanity is currently facing, such as achieving the global sustainability goals. Today's Edisonian approach of trial-and-error still prevalent in R&D labs takes up to two decades of fundamental and applied research for new materials to reach the market. Turning around this situation calls for strategies to upgrade R&D and expedite innovation. By conducting smart experiment planning that is data-driven and guided by AI/ML, researchers can more efficiently search through the complex - often constrained - space of possible experiments and find or hit the global optima much faster than with the current approaches. Moreover, with digitized data management, researchers will be able to maximize the utility of their data in the short and long terms with the aid of statistics, ML and visualization tools. In what follows, we describe a framework and lay out the key technologies to accelerate R&D and optimize experiment planning.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Large Language Models (LLMs) have seen rapid advancements in recent years, with models like ChatGPT and DeepSeek, showcasing their remarkable capabilities across diverse domains. While substantial research has been conducted on LLMs in various fields, a comprehensive review focusing on their integration with Computer-Aided Design (CAD) remains notably absent. CAD is the industry standard for 3D modeling and plays a vital role in the design and development of products across different industries. As the complexity of modern designs increases, the potential for LLMs to enhance and streamline CAD workflows presents an exciting frontier. This article presents the first systematic survey exploring the intersection of LLMs and CAD. We begin by outlining the industrial significance of CAD, highlighting the need for Artificial Intelligence (AI)-driven innovation. Next, we provide a detailed overview of the foundation of LLMs. We also examine both closed-source LLMs as well as publicly available models. The core of this review focuses on the various applications of LLMs in CAD, providing a taxonomy of six key areas where these models are making considerable impact. We also provide a comprehensive study of CAD evaluation, reviewing existing methods and metrics in detail. In our analysis, we also examine common data modalities, model usage trends, dataset sources, and industrial application domains to provide a well-rounded picture of the field. Finally, we propose several promising future directions for further advancements, which offer vast opportunities for innovation and are poised to shape the future of CAD technology. Github: https://github.com/lichengzhanguom/LLMs-CAD-Survey-Taxonomy.</jats:p>

## 5. CAD-MLLM: Unifying Multimodality-Conditioned CAD Generation With MLLM
Valid Text Names:
'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 1-2', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 2-3', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 3-4', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 4-5', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 5-6', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 6-8', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 8-9', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 9-10', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 10-11', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 11-12', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 12-13', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 13-15', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 15-16', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 16-17', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 16-16', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 17-18', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 18-19', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 18-18', 'xu2024cadmllmunifyingmultimodalityconditioned pages 19-19'

    author = "Xu, Jingwei and Wang, Chenyu and Zhao, Zibo and Liu, Wen and Ma, Yi and Gao, Shenghua",
    title = "CAD-MLLM: Unifying Multimodality-Conditioned CAD Generation With MLLM",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2411.04954",
    month = "Nov",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2411.04954",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2411.04954",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
This paper aims to design a unified Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generation system that can easily generate CAD models based on the user's inputs in the form of textual description, images, point clouds, or even a combination of them. Towards this goal, we introduce the CAD-MLLM, the first system capable of generating parametric CAD models conditioned on the multimodal input. Specifically, within the CAD-MLLM framework, we leverage the command sequences of CAD models and then employ advanced large language models (LLMs) to align the feature space across these diverse multi-modalities data and CAD models'vectorized representations. To facilitate the model training, we design a comprehensive data construction and annotation pipeline that equips each CAD model with corresponding multimodal data. Our resulting dataset, named Omni-CAD, is the first multimodal CAD dataset that contains textual description, multi-view images, points, and command sequence for each CAD model. It contains approximately 450K instances and their CAD construction sequences. To thoroughly evaluate the quality of our generated CAD models, we go beyond current evaluation metrics that focus on reconstruction quality by introducing additional metrics that assess topology quality and surface enclosure extent. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that CAD-MLLM significantly outperforms existing conditional generative methods and remains highly robust to noises and missing points. The project page and more visualizations can be found at: https://cad-mllm.github.io/

Abstract:
<jats:p>Generating editable 3D CAD models from natural language remains challenging, as existing text-to-CAD systems either produce meshes or rely on scarce design-history data. We present NURBGen, the first framework to generate high fidelity 3D CAD models directly from text using Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines (NURBS). To achieve this, we fine-tune a large language model (LLM) to translate free-form texts into JSON representations containing NURBS surface parameters (i.e, control points, knot vectors, degrees, and rational weights) which can be directly converted into BRep format using Python. We further propose a hybrid representation that combines untrimmed NURBS with analytic primitives to handle trimmed surfaces and degenerate regions more robustly, while reducing token complexity. Additionally, we introduce partABC, a curated subset of the ABC dataset consisting of individual CAD components, annotated with detailed captions using an automated annotation pipeline. NURBGen demonstrates strong performance on diverse prompts, surpassing prior methods in geometric fidelity and dimensional accuracy, as confirmed by expert evaluations.</jats:p>

## 8. CAD-Recode: Reverse Engineering CAD Code from Point Clouds

Abstract:
Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models are typically constructed by sequentially drawing parametric sketches and applying CAD operations to obtain a 3D model. The problem of 3D CAD reverse engineering consists of reconstructing the sketch and CAD operation sequences from 3D representations such as point clouds. In this paper, we address this challenge through novel contributions across three levels: CAD sequence representation, network design, and training dataset. In particular, we represent CAD sketch-extrude sequences as Python code. The proposed CAD-Recode translates a point cloud into Python code that, when executed, reconstructs the CAD model. Taking advantage of the exposure of pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) to Python code, we leverage a relatively small LLM as a decoder for CAD-Recode and combine it with a lightweight point cloud projector. CAD-Recode is trained on a procedurally generated dataset of one million CAD sequences. CAD-Recode significantly outperforms existing methods across the DeepCAD, Fusion360 and real-world CC3D datasets. Furthermore, we show that our CAD Python code output is interpretable by off-the-shelf LLMs, enabling CAD editing and CAD-specific question answering from point clouds.

## 10. From 2D to 3D Cognition: A Brief Survey of General World Models

Abstract:
World models have garnered increasing attention in the development of artificial general intelligence (AGI), serving as computational frameworks for learning representations of the external world and forecasting future states. While early efforts focused on 2D visual perception and simulation, recent 3D-aware generative world models have demonstrated the ability to synthesize geometrically consistent, interactive 3D environments, marking a shift toward 3D spatial cognition. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks systematic analysis to categorize emerging techniques and clarify their roles in advancing 3D cognitive world models. This survey addresses this need by introducing a conceptual framework, providing a structured and forward-looking review of world models transitioning from 2D perception to 3D cognition. Within this framework, we highlight two key technological drivers, particularly advances in 3D representations and the incorporation of world knowledge, as fundamental pillars. Building on these, we dissect three core cognitive capabilities that underpin 3D world modeling: 3D physical scene generation, 3D spatial reasoning, and 3D spatial interaction. We further examine the deployment of these capabilities in real-world applications, including embodied AI, autonomous driving, digital twin, and gaming/VR. Finally, we identify challenges across data, modeling, and deployment, and outline future directions for advancing more robust and generalizable 3D world models.

## 12. Can Large Language Models Understand Symbolic Graphics Programs?
Valid Text Names:
'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 1-2', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 2-3', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 3-3', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 4-4', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 6-8', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 8-8', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 9-9', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 30-30', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 31-31', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 32-32', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 33-33', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 36-36', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 37-37', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 37-38', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 38-40', 'qiu2024canlargelanguage pages 38-38'

    title = "Can Large Language Models Understand Symbolic Graphics Programs?",
    year = "2025",

Abstract:
Against the backdrop of enthusiasm for large language models (LLMs), there is a growing need to scientifically assess their capabilities and shortcomings. This is nontrivial in part because it is difficult to find tasks which the models have not encountered during training. Utilizing symbolic graphics programs, we propose a domain well-suited to test multiple spatial-semantic reasoning skills of LLMs. Popular in computer graphics, these programs procedurally generate visual data. While LLMs exhibit impressive skills in general program synthesis and analysis, symbolic graphics programs offer a new layer of evaluation: they allow us to test an LLM's ability to answer semantic questions about the images or 3D geometries without a vision encoder. To semantically understand the symbolic programs, LLMs would need to possess the ability to"imagine"and reason how the corresponding graphics content would look with only the symbolic description of the local curvatures and strokes. We use this task to evaluate LLMs by creating a large benchmark for the semantic visual understanding of symbolic graphics programs, built procedurally with minimal human effort. Particular emphasis is placed on transformations of images that leave the image level semantics invariant while introducing significant changes to the underlying program. We evaluate commercial and open-source LLMs on our benchmark to assess their ability to reason about visual output of programs, finding that LLMs considered stronger at reasoning generally perform better. Lastly, we introduce a novel method to improve this ability -- Symbolic Instruction Tuning (SIT), in which the LLM is finetuned with pre-collected instruction data on symbolic graphics programs. Interestingly, we find that SIT not only improves LLM's understanding on symbolic programs, but it also improves general reasoning ability on various other benchmarks.

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive tier-ranked table of conferences, workshops, and symposia for presenting the open-hardware SDL multi-powder doser with generative-CAD pipeline. Group into three tiers: Best-Fit, Strong, and Stretch. For each venue include columns: Tier, Venue Full Name, Organizer, Scope, Typical Timing/Location, Submission Format, Selectivity/Prestige, and Fit Justification (1-3 sentences).

BEST-FIT TIER venues (SDL + hardware + open-hardware + materials):
1. Accelerate Conference (Acceleration Consortium) - annual, SDL community, workshops + demos, abstract/poster, Toronto/Vancouver
2. Faraday Discussions (RSC) - data-driven discovery topics, full paper + discussion published, high prestige
3. MRS Fall Meeting - symposia on autonomous experimentation, abstract-based, Boston, Dec
4. SFF Symposium (Solid Freeform Fabrication) - UT Austin, Aug, full papers, AM-focused, open proceedings
5. ORNL "Shaping the Future of SDL" Workshop series - DOE/ORNL, Nov 2024 Denver, breakout sessions

STRONG TIER venues:
6. TMS Annual Meeting - alloy/metallurgy focus, abstract-based, Spring
7. MRS Spring Meeting - Phoenix, April, abstract
8. ASME IDETC/CIE (DAC + CIE tracks) - full papers, Aug, design automation + computing
9. AIChE Annual Meeting - automation + process, abstract/paper, Nov
10. AI4Mat Workshop at NeurIPS - non-archival, 4-8 page workshop papers, Dec
11. AI4Mat Workshop at ICLR - non-archival, similar format, Spring
12. GOSH (Gathering for Open Science Hardware) - unconference, open participation
13. ASTM ICAM - AM standards, abstract, annual
14. RAPID + TCT - SME, industry expo + presentations, abstract

STRETCH TIER venues:
15. CVPR workshops (3D generation, design) - archival workshop papers, ~20% acceptance
16. SIGGRAPH - technical papers, ~25% acceptance, ACM
17. NeurIPS main conference - highly selective, primarily ML
18. ICML workshops - non-archival, ML focused
19. GRC on Combinatorial/HT Materials Science - by-invitation poster + discussion
20. ICLR main conference - highly selective ML venue"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Tier | Venue Full Name | Organizer | Scope | Typical Timing/Location | Submission Format | Selectivity/Prestige | Fit Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best-Fit | Accelerate Conference | Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto | Self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, autonomous experimentation, community demos/workshops | Annual; recent editions in Toronto/Vancouver; often summer-fall; exact future location/year can vary | Typically abstract/poster/demo/workshop-style participation; usually non-archival or lightly archival; workshop/demonstration friendly | High relevance inside SDL community; prestige is field-specific rather than society-wide | Probably the single best audience for an open-hardware SDL doser because the community already values low-cost, user-developed automation infrastructure, calibration workflows, and democratized hardware. It is also unusually receptive to system demonstrations and practical infrastructure rather than only algorithmic novelty. (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001) |
| Best-Fit | Faraday Discussions (especially data-driven discovery / AI-for-chemistry-materials topics) | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | High-level themed meetings on chemistry/materials topics; for this project, data-driven discovery, automation, AI, and self-driving-lab adjacent themes are most relevant | Topic-dependent; held in the UK and internationally; cadence depends on theme rather than one fixed annual slot | Distinctive pre-submitted paper plus oral discussion model; archival in *Faraday Discussions* with discussion record published | Very prestigious, small, curated, discussion-intensive | Best if the manuscript is framed around closed-loop gravimetric calibration, autonomous materials-discovery infrastructure, and lessons for data-driven experimentation. Less ideal for purely mechanical build details, but excellent for a polished SDL systems paper aligned with *Digital Discovery* and RSC audiences. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022) |
| Best-Fit | Materials Research Society (MRS) Fall Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials meeting with recurring symposia on materials informatics, autonomous experimentation, AI for materials, and advanced manufacturing | Annual; typically late Nov/Dec in Boston, MA | Usually abstract-based talks/posters; archival proceedings uncommon for most symposia | Very high prestige in materials science; broad and visible | Strong fit for the alloy-discovery and SDL materials angle, especially if submitted to a symposium on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or manufacturing/data infrastructure. Hardware-heavy AM dosing can stand out if coupled to compelling calibration data and a clear autonomous-discovery use case. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010) |
| Best-Fit | Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium (SFF Symposium) – An Additive Manufacturing Conference | The University of Texas at Austin | Deeply technical additive manufacturing conference spanning processes, feedstocks, design, materials, and machine development | Annual; typically August; Austin, Texas | Full paper in proceedings; archival/openly accessible conference papers are a norm for many contributions | High prestige in AM research, especially for technical depth and engineering rigor | Excellent venue for the AM hardware side: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, and integration into AM workflows. It is better than most AM venues for a detailed engineering paper because the audience accepts apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions. (pqac-00000015) |
| Best-Fit | Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories Workshop | Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) / DOE-lab ecosystem | National-lab-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, workforce, and ecosystem building | Recent documented edition: Nov. 7–8, 2024, Denver, CO; future continuity is plausible but specific series recurrence is unverified | Workshop participation via talks/panels/breakouts; non-archival meeting format documented in technical report | High strategic relevance; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven | Outstanding fit if the goal is feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab and infrastructure builders. The project’s open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration/control aspects align especially well with the workshop’s instrumentation, interoperability, and standards focus. (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) |
| Strong | The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS) Annual Meeting & Exhibition | TMS | Metals, alloys, ICME, process metallurgy, manufacturing, and data-enabled materials engineering | Annual; typically February-March; rotating North American cities | Usually abstract-based presentations; some tracks lead to proceedings or journal special issues | High prestige in metallurgy and structural materials | Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience, especially if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys. Less naturally open-hardware-focused than SDL meetings, but stronger on alloy relevance than many automation venues. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) |
| Strong | Materials Research Society (MRS) Spring Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials conference with symposia on informatics, automation, energy materials, and emerging fabrication methods | Annual; typically April; often Phoenix, AZ or Seattle, WA | Usually abstract-only talk/poster submissions; mostly non-archival | Very high prestige in materials science | Similar benefits to MRS Fall, though often a bit less central for some physical-metallurgy communities. A good option if there is a spring symposium specifically on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or digital manufacturing. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010) |
| Strong | ASME International Design Engineering Technical Conferences & Computers and Information in Engineering Conference (IDETC/CIE), especially DAC and CIE tracks | American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) | Engineering design, design automation, computational design, CAD/CAM, engineering informatics, AI-enabled design methods | Annual; typically August; recent documented edition Aug. 17–20, 2025 in Anaheim, CA | Full papers in ASME conference proceedings; archival | Strong prestige in engineering design and design automation | Best venue for the generative-CAD half if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. It is not the strongest SDL venue, but it is the best serious archival design-engineering venue on your list for the CAD contribution. (pqac-00000014) |
| Strong | AIChE Annual Meeting | American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) | Chemical engineering, process systems, laboratory automation, robotics, optimization, digitalization, autonomous experimentation | Annual; typically November; rotating U.S. cities | Usually abstract-based conference talks; some invited or extended submissions; mostly non-archival | High prestige in chemical/process engineering | Good fit if the work is framed as a dosing/dispensing platform, process-control problem, or autonomous experimentation module rather than primarily as AM hardware. Hardware rigor and closed-loop calibration can resonate, but AM-alloy relevance may be less central than at TMS/SFF. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000010) |
| Strong | AI4Mat Workshop at NeurIPS | Workshop organizers under the NeurIPS workshop program; AI4Mat community | AI for accelerated materials design, materials discovery, inverse design, benchmarks, tools, and findings | At NeurIPS in December; location follows NeurIPS host city | Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview; categories have included papers, tools & findings, tiny papers, and themed tracks | High visibility workshop attached to elite ML venue; workshop prestige is strong but below main conference | Good fit only if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, data/benchmark value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline as a methodological contribution. Hardware-heavy apparatus papers are less central here, but the workshop is notably friendlier to tools and findings than most ML venues. (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| Strong | AI4Mat Workshop at ICLR | Workshop organizers under the ICLR workshop program; AI4Mat community | AI for accelerated materials design with emphasis on ML methods, data, tools, and interdisciplinary materials applications | At ICLR in spring; location follows ICLR host city | Non-archival workshop papers, tiny papers, findings/tools tracks, OpenReview-based review | High visibility workshop; somewhat more methods-focused than hardware-focused | Similar to AI4Mat-NeurIPS, but potentially slightly easier to place interdisciplinary tool papers. Best if you stress the generative-CAD pipeline or autonomous design loop rather than only the electromechanical doser build. (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003) |
| Strong | Gathering for Open Science Hardware (GOSH) | Global Open Science Hardware community | Open-source/open-science hardware, documentation, accessibility, community building, inclusive hardware practices | Irregular but recurring global gatherings; location varies; participation model is community-driven | Unconference/workshops/sessions rather than formal archival papers; open participation and hands-on sessions | High reputation within open-hardware community; low conventional academic selectivity | Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and community adoption. It is weaker as a conventional academic publication venue, but few places are better for finding users, contributors, and best practices for sharing the doser design responsibly and reproducibly. (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013) |
| Strong | ASTM International Conference on Additive Manufacturing (ICAM) | ASTM International, often with AM Center of Excellence partners | Additive manufacturing processes, feedstocks, qualification, standards, industrial adoption, materials and testing | Annual; timing/location vary internationally | Usually abstract-based presentations; proceedings/publication pathways vary by year | Strong applied/industrial prestige, especially for standards and qualification | A good venue if the paper is framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, calibration, and standardizable AM feedstock preparation infrastructure. Less ideal for the generative-CAD story, but strong for translation toward industrial AM practice. (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000015) |
| Strong | RAPID + TCT | SME in partnership with TCT | Major additive manufacturing event combining research, applications, and industry exhibition | Annual; typically spring in North America; location varies | Usually abstract-based conference presentations; many talks are non-archival | High visibility in industrial AM; academic selectivity moderate | Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization, user adoption, or system demos matter. It is less archival and less materials-discovery-centric than SFF, so it ranks below SFF for this paper. (pqac-00000015) |
| Stretch | CVPR Workshops relevant to 3D/CAD generation | IEEE/CVF CVPR workshop organizers | Computer vision, 3D generation, multimodal design, geometry, CAD-related generation/reconstruction | With CVPR, typically June; location follows host city | Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies by workshop | Selective and visible in ML/vision; fit depends heavily on workshop theme | Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, especially if you have clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation, multimodal conditioning, or CAD sequence modeling. The SDL powder-doser hardware itself will not be the main attraction. (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000027) |
| Stretch | SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM Transactions on Graphics pathway | ACM SIGGRAPH / ACM | Top-tier graphics, geometry processing, generative shape/CAD modeling, 3D representations | Annual; typically summer; rotating North American and international venues | Full technical paper; archival, highly selective | Elite venue in graphics/geometry | Relevant only if the generative-CAD component is a substantial standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations, B-rep generation, or high-impact geometry learning. For a hardware-first paper, this is mainly a stretch side-channel rather than a primary target. (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025) |
| Stretch | Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) – main conference | NeurIPS Foundation | Core machine learning conference with theory, methods, and AI-for-science papers | Annual; typically December; rotating locations | Full archival conference paper | Extremely selective and prestigious | A stretch unless the work’s main novelty is an ML method, benchmark, or broadly interesting AI system rather than the doser hardware. The materials/SDL framing is better suited to the workshop ecosystem than to the main conference. (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000001) |
| Stretch | ICML Workshops relevant to AI for Science / materials / design | Workshop organizers under the ICML program | ML methods, AI for science, scientific discovery, sometimes design/generative modeling | With ICML, typically July; location follows host city | Usually non-archival workshop papers or abstracts | Good visibility but workshop-level prestige | Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL- or materials-specific meetings. Best as a side venue for the AI/design methodology, not the main apparatus paper. (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000003) |
| Stretch | Gordon Research Conference on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science | Gordon Research Conferences | Intensive, discussion-focused meeting on high-throughput and combinatorial materials science | Typically summer at residential venues; by application; often in North America/Europe | Application-based attendance; posters common; no formal archival proceedings | Very prestigious, intimate, and discussion-driven | Scientifically very strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if your goal is a citable conference paper or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building with senior materials researchers once results are mature. (pqac-00000008) |
| Stretch | International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) – main conference | ICLR organizing body | Core ML conference centered on representation learning and foundation models | Annual; typically spring; rotating location | Full archival conference paper via OpenReview | Extremely selective and prestigious | Like NeurIPS main, this is only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant scientific contribution and is competitive as ML research independent of the doser. The hardware SDL story by itself is not a natural fit. (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000003) |


*Table: This table ranks the most relevant presentation venues for the open-hardware self-driving-lab multi-powder doser and its generative-CAD pipeline. It balances SDL/materials fit, additive-manufacturing relevance, and generative-CAD visibility while noting submission style and prestige.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027]

function.name: update_plan

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
