*Table: This table ranks the most relevant presentation venues for the open-hardware self-driving-lab multi-powder doser and its generative-CAD pipeline. It balances SDL/materials fit, additive-manufacturing relevance, and generative-CAD visibility while noting submission style and prestige.*

---

## Tier 1: Best-Fit Venues (Highest Priority)

### 1. Accelerate Conference (Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto)

- **Scope:** The SDL community's flagship event, covering self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, open/democratized infrastructure, and autonomous experimentation workflows.
- **Timing/Location:** Annual; the 2024 edition was held in Vancouver, BC (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3).
- **Submission format:** Workshop-style participation including talks, demonstrations, poster sessions, and community showcases. The "Democratizing Self-Driving Labs" workshop at Accelerate 2024 featured 14 contributed hardware/software demonstrations (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). Generally non-archival or lightly archival.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High within the SDL community; field-specific rather than society-wide. The Acceleration Consortium is led by Alán Aspuru-Guzik at the University of Toronto (verifiable at https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/).
- **Fit justification:** This is the single best audience for the doser. The Democratizing SDLs workshop explicitly celebrated user-developed hardware infrastructure, cost-driven open-source designs, and practical SDL building blocks (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). The multi-powder doser with its open-hardware, generative-CAD, and calibration contributions maps directly onto their themes.
- **Official page:** https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/accelerate

### 2. Faraday Discussions (RSC) — AI/Data-Driven Topics

- **Scope:** The Royal Society of Chemistry's prestigious discussion-meeting series. A recent Faraday Discussion on "Data-driven discovery in the chemical sciences" (Vol. 256, 2025) covered ML, automation, robotics, and self-driving laboratories extensively (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 13-16, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 11-13). Papers on SDLs for optoelectronic materials have appeared in the same series (scragg2026).
- **Timing/Location:** Topic-dependent scheduling; held in the UK and internationally. Meetings are small and curated.
- **Submission format:** Full papers are submitted in advance and pre-published; oral discussion at the meeting is recorded and published alongside the papers in the *Faraday Discussions* journal (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26). This is a distinctive, archival, high-prestige format.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high. Faraday Discussions papers carry significant weight. Invitations are typically extended by the scientific committee.
- **Fit justification:** Ideal if the paper emphasizes closed-loop calibration as an autonomous-experimentation infrastructure contribution, data-driven workflow design, or lessons for accelerating materials discovery. This aligns directly with the primary target journal *Digital Discovery* (RSC).
- **Official page:** https://www.rsc.org/events/series/faraday-discussions

### 3. MRS Fall Meeting (Materials Research Society)

- **Scope:** The largest annual materials science conference, with recurring symposia on autonomous experimentation, materials informatics, AI-accelerated materials discovery, and advanced manufacturing (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7).
- **Timing/Location:** Late November/December, Boston, MA.
- **Submission format:** Abstract-based oral talks and posters. Proceedings are uncommon for most symposia; contributions are typically non-archival at the conference level.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high prestige in materials science.
- **Fit justification:** Strong for the alloy-discovery and autonomous-experimentation framing. Look for symposia titled along the lines of "Autonomous Experimentation," "Materials Acceleration Platforms," or "AI for Materials." Hardware-rich contributions with compelling calibration data and an autonomous discovery use case can stand out.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/fall-meeting

### 4. Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF) Symposium — An Additive Manufacturing Conference

- **Scope:** The premier academic AM conference covering processes, feedstocks, machine development, materials characterization, and design for AM (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
- **Timing/Location:** Annual, typically August, hosted by the University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX.
- **Submission format:** Full papers in open-access proceedings published by UT Austin. The proceedings are openly available and widely cited in the AM community.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High prestige in AM research, particularly for technical depth.
- **Fit justification:** Excellent for the AM hardware side of the project: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, auger mechanisms, and integration into AM workflows. SFF is more accepting of apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions than many other AM venues (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
- **Official page:** https://www.sffsymposium.org/

### 5. "Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories" Workshop (ORNL / DOE)

- **Scope:** A U.S. national-laboratory-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, and ecosystem building (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
- **Timing/Location:** The inaugural edition was held November 7–8, 2024, in Denver, CO (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17). Future continuity is plausible but not yet confirmed as a recurring series.
- **Submission format:** Participation via talks, panels, and interactive breakout sessions. The workshop output is documented in an ORNL technical report (ORNL/TM-2024/3714) rather than archival proceedings (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4).
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High strategic value; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven. Participants represent ORNL, SLAC, PNNL, CMU, NCSU, JHU, UTK, Sandia, and other institutions (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
- **Fit justification:** Outstanding for gaining feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab infrastructure builders. The project's open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration aspects align with the workshop's instrumentation and interoperability focus.
- **Official page:** The 2024 report is at https://doi.org/10.2172/2481197

---

## Tier 2: Strong Venues

### 6. TMS Annual Meeting & Exhibition
- **Organizer:** The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS). **Timing:** Annually, Feb–Mar, rotating North American cities. **Format:** Abstract-based talks/posters; some symposia produce proceedings or journal special issues. **Prestige:** High in metallurgy and structural materials. **Fit:** Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys.
- **Official page:** https://www.tms.org/AnnualMeeting

### 7. MRS Spring Meeting
- **Organizer:** Materials Research Society. **Timing:** Annually, April, often Phoenix, AZ. **Format:** Abstract-based; non-archival. **Prestige:** Very high. **Fit:** Similar to MRS Fall; look for spring symposia on autonomous experimentation or digital manufacturing.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/spring-meeting

### 8. ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference (DAC) and Computers and Information in Engineering (CIE)
- **Organizer:** American Society of Mechanical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually in August; 2025 edition was August 17–20 in Anaheim, CA (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Format:** Full archival papers in ASME conference proceedings. The DAC track (Vol. 3A/B) and CIE track (Vol. 2) are separate sub-conferences within the larger IDETC/CIE event (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Prestige:** Strong in engineering design and design automation. **Fit:** The best serious archival design-engineering venue for the generative-CAD half, especially if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. Recent work on generative CAD and AI copilots for design has appeared here.
- **Official page:** https://event.asme.org/IDETC-CIE

### 9. AIChE Annual Meeting
- **Organizer:** American Institute of Chemical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually, November. **Format:** Abstract-based; mostly non-archival. **Prestige:** High in chemical/process engineering. **Fit:** Good for the dosing/dispensing and process-control framing, autonomous experimentation modules, or closed-loop optimization.
- **Official page:** https://www.aiche.org/annual

### 10–11. AI4Mat Workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR
- **Organizer:** AI4Mat community (organizers include S. Miret, D. Circi, N.M.A. Krishnan, E. Jin) (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Timing:** AI4Mat-NeurIPS runs at NeurIPS in December; AI4Mat-ICLR runs at ICLR in spring. The series has run at NeurIPS 2022–2025 (growing from ~60 to ~250 participants) and at ICLR 2025–2026 (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2). **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview (at least 3 reviewers per submission); submission categories include Papers, Tools & Findings, Tiny Papers (2-page extended abstracts), and themed tracks (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Prestige:** High visibility within the ML-for-materials community. **Fit:** Good if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, benchmark/data value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline. The "Tools & Findings" track is notably friendlier to infrastructure and hardware than most ML venues.
- **Official page:** https://sites.google.com/view/ai4mat (check for current edition URL)

### 12. GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware
- **Organizer:** Global Open Science Hardware community. **Timing:** Irregular but recurring global gatherings since 2016; locations have included Shenzhen (2018) and other international sites (arancio2021openingupthe pages 20-23). **Format:** Unconference/open-participation model; ~70% of the program is defined on-demand; includes hands-on workshops, building sessions, and community deliberation. Attendance is open, often with subsidized participation (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12). No formal archival proceedings. **Prestige:** High within the open-hardware movement; low conventional academic selectivity. **Fit:** Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and adoption. Few places are better for finding users and contributors for the doser design.
- **Official page:** https://openhardware.science/

### 13. ASTM ICAM (International Conference on Additive Manufacturing)
- **Organizer:** ASTM International, with AM Center of Excellence partners. **Timing:** Annual, varying international locations. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations. **Prestige:** Strong for standards and qualification in AM. **Fit:** Good if framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, and standardizable AM feedstock infrastructure.
- **Official page:** https://amcoe.org/icam

### 14. RAPID + TCT
- **Organizer:** SME in partnership with TCT. **Timing:** Annual, typically spring, North America. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations; many talks are non-archival. Industry exhibition is a major component. **Prestige:** High visibility in industrial AM; moderate academic selectivity. **Fit:** Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization or system demos matter. Less archival than SFF.
- **Official page:** https://www.rapid3devent.com/

---

## Tier 3: Stretch Venues

### 15. CVPR Workshops (3D/CAD Generation)
- **Organizer:** IEEE/CVF. **Timing:** Annually, typically June. **Format:** Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies. **Prestige:** High in ML/vision. **Fit:** Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, if there is clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation. CAD-Llama appeared at CVPR 2025 (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2), and CADLoop appeared at a CVPR workshop (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11). The SDL hardware itself will not be the main attraction.
- **Official page:** https://cvpr.thecvf.com/

### 16. SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM TOG
- **Organizer:** ACM SIGGRAPH. **Timing:** Annually, summer. **Format:** Full archival technical papers; highly selective (~25% acceptance). **Prestige:** Elite in graphics/geometry. BrepGen was published in ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 8-9). **Fit:** Only if the generative-CAD component is a standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations or geometry generation methods.
- **Official page:** https://www.siggraph.org/

### 17. NeurIPS Main Conference
- **Organizer:** NeurIPS Foundation. **Timing:** Annually, December. **Format:** Full archival conference paper; extremely selective (~20–25%). **Fit:** A stretch unless the work's main novelty is an ML method or benchmark. The workshop ecosystem (AI4Mat) is far more appropriate for this project.
- **Official page:** https://neurips.cc/

### 18. ICML Workshops (AI for Science / Design)
- **Organizer:** ICML workshop organizers. **Timing:** Annually, July. **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers. **Fit:** Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL-specific meetings.
- **Official page:** https://icml.cc/

### 19. GRC on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science
- **Organizer:** Gordon Research Conferences. **Timing:** Typically summer, at residential venues. **Format:** Application-based attendance; posters and short talks; no archival proceedings. **Prestige:** Very prestigious and intimate. **Fit:** Scientifically strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if the goal is a citable output or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building once results are mature.
- **Official page:** https://www.grc.org/ (search for "Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science")

### 20. ICLR Main Conference
- **Organizer:** ICLR. **Timing:** Annually, spring. **Format:** Full archival papers via OpenReview; extremely selective. **Fit:** Only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant contribution competitive as ML research. The hardware SDL story is not a natural fit for the main conference.
- **Official page:** https://iclr.cc/

---

## Strategic Recommendations

1. **Lead with SDL-focused venues.** The Accelerate Conference and the ORNL SDL workshop series are the most natural homes for the integrated hardware + autonomous-experimentation story. These communities explicitly value open-hardware, user-developed automation infrastructure, and practical SDL building blocks (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4).

2. **Target MRS Fall and SFF for materials and AM credibility.** For peer recognition in materials science and additive manufacturing, an MRS Fall symposium on autonomous experimentation or an SFF paper on the AM hardware side provides high visibility and archival value (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).

3. **Use ASME IDETC/CIE for the generative-CAD angle.** The DAC and CIE tracks within IDETC/CIE are the most appropriate archival engineering-design venues for the LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). Recent work on AI copilots for design, CAD-prompted generative models, and generative statistically self-similar structures has appeared here.

4. **Consider Faraday Discussions for a polished, high-prestige presentation.** If a future Faraday Discussion is announced on automation, AI for materials, or data-driven discovery, the project would fit well as a pre-submitted paper emphasizing the closed-loop calibration and autonomous discovery infrastructure (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26).

5. **Use AI4Mat workshop "Tools & Findings" tracks for ML-community visibility.** The AI4Mat workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR have specific tracks for tools, infrastructure, and findings that are more accommodating of hardware-rich contributions than typical ML venues (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4).

6. **GOSH for community building and adoption.** While GOSH does not produce archival papers, it is unmatched for building a user community around the open-hardware doser design and getting feedback on documentation, reproducibility, and accessibility (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12).

7. **Stretch ML/graphics venues only with a strong standalone ML contribution.** CVPR, SIGGRAPH, NeurIPS, and ICLR main conferences are realistic only if the generative-CAD pipeline yields novel ML methodology that stands on its own. For LLM-based CAD generation, CVPR workshops and AAAI have been receptive (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 7-8, li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2).

---

## Note on Contact Information

Per the user's instructions, no email addresses or personal contact details are fabricated. Public verification of individuals mentioned in this document (e.g., Alán Aspuru-Guzik, Andrew Cooper, Santiago Miret) can be confirmed via their institutional faculty pages, Google Scholar profiles, or the official conference/workshop pages cited above. No personal contact information was found to be necessary beyond the institutional and conference pages already provided.

## Overview

The project—an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy discovery, featuring closed-loop gravimetric calibration, an auger auto-change mechanism, 50-powder capacity, and an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline—sits at the intersection of several communities: (1) self-driving-lab / autonomous experimentation, (2) materials science and metallurgy, (3) additive manufacturing, (4) engineering design automation and generative CAD, and (5) open science hardware. Below, venues are assessed, tier-ranked, and annotated with detailed metadata. Venues are grouped into **Best-Fit**, **Strong**, and **Stretch** tiers based on their alignment with a hardware-focused SDL contribution.

*Table: This table ranks the most relevant presentation venues for the open-hardware self-driving-lab multi-powder doser and its generative-CAD pipeline. It balances SDL/materials fit, additive-manufacturing relevance, and generative-CAD visibility while noting submission style and prestige.*

---

## Tier 1: Best-Fit Venues (Highest Priority)

### 1. Accelerate Conference (Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto)

- **Scope:** The SDL community's flagship event, covering self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, open/democratized infrastructure, and autonomous experimentation workflows.
- **Timing/Location:** Annual; the 2024 edition was held in Vancouver, BC (pqac-00000000).
- **Submission format:** Workshop-style participation including talks, demonstrations, poster sessions, and community showcases. The "Democratizing Self-Driving Labs" workshop at Accelerate 2024 featured 14 contributed hardware/software demonstrations (pqac-00000000). Generally non-archival or lightly archival.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High within the SDL community; field-specific rather than society-wide. The Acceleration Consortium is led by Alán Aspuru-Guzik at the University of Toronto (verifiable at https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/).
- **Fit justification:** This is the single best audience for the doser. The Democratizing SDLs workshop explicitly celebrated user-developed hardware infrastructure, cost-driven open-source designs, and practical SDL building blocks (pqac-00000000). The multi-powder doser with its open-hardware, generative-CAD, and calibration contributions maps directly onto their themes.
- **Official page:** https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/accelerate

### 2. Faraday Discussions (RSC) — AI/Data-Driven Topics

- **Scope:** The Royal Society of Chemistry's prestigious discussion-meeting series. A recent Faraday Discussion on "Data-driven discovery in the chemical sciences" (Vol. 256, 2025) covered ML, automation, robotics, and self-driving laboratories extensively (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018). Papers on SDLs for optoelectronic materials have appeared in the same series (scragg2026).
- **Timing/Location:** Topic-dependent scheduling; held in the UK and internationally. Meetings are small and curated.
- **Submission format:** Full papers are submitted in advance and pre-published; oral discussion at the meeting is recorded and published alongside the papers in the *Faraday Discussions* journal (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000022). This is a distinctive, archival, high-prestige format.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high. Faraday Discussions papers carry significant weight. Invitations are typically extended by the scientific committee.
- **Fit justification:** Ideal if the paper emphasizes closed-loop calibration as an autonomous-experimentation infrastructure contribution, data-driven workflow design, or lessons for accelerating materials discovery. This aligns directly with the primary target journal *Digital Discovery* (RSC).
- **Official page:** https://www.rsc.org/events/series/faraday-discussions

### 3. MRS Fall Meeting (Materials Research Society)

- **Scope:** The largest annual materials science conference, with recurring symposia on autonomous experimentation, materials informatics, AI-accelerated materials discovery, and advanced manufacturing (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010).
- **Timing/Location:** Late November/December, Boston, MA.
- **Submission format:** Abstract-based oral talks and posters. Proceedings are uncommon for most symposia; contributions are typically non-archival at the conference level.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high prestige in materials science.
- **Fit justification:** Strong for the alloy-discovery and autonomous-experimentation framing. Look for symposia titled along the lines of "Autonomous Experimentation," "Materials Acceleration Platforms," or "AI for Materials." Hardware-rich contributions with compelling calibration data and an autonomous discovery use case can stand out.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/fall-meeting

### 4. Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF) Symposium — An Additive Manufacturing Conference

- **Scope:** The premier academic AM conference covering processes, feedstocks, machine development, materials characterization, and design for AM (pqac-00000015).
- **Timing/Location:** Annual, typically August, hosted by the University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX.
- **Submission format:** Full papers in open-access proceedings published by UT Austin. The proceedings are openly available and widely cited in the AM community.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High prestige in AM research, particularly for technical depth.
- **Fit justification:** Excellent for the AM hardware side of the project: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, auger mechanisms, and integration into AM workflows. SFF is more accepting of apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions than many other AM venues (pqac-00000015).
- **Official page:** https://www.sffsymposium.org/

### 5. "Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories" Workshop (ORNL / DOE)

- **Scope:** A U.S. national-laboratory-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, and ecosystem building (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006).
- **Timing/Location:** The inaugural edition was held November 7–8, 2024, in Denver, CO (pqac-00000005). Future continuity is plausible but not yet confirmed as a recurring series.
- **Submission format:** Participation via talks, panels, and interactive breakout sessions. The workshop output is documented in an ORNL technical report (ORNL/TM-2024/3714) rather than archival proceedings (pqac-00000004).
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High strategic value; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven. Participants represent ORNL, SLAC, PNNL, CMU, NCSU, JHU, UTK, Sandia, and other institutions (pqac-00000006).
- **Fit justification:** Outstanding for gaining feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab infrastructure builders. The project's open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration aspects align with the workshop's instrumentation and interoperability focus.
- **Official page:** The 2024 report is at https://doi.org/10.2172/2481197

---

## Tier 2: Strong Venues

### 6. TMS Annual Meeting & Exhibition
- **Organizer:** The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS). **Timing:** Annually, Feb–Mar, rotating North American cities. **Format:** Abstract-based talks/posters; some symposia produce proceedings or journal special issues. **Prestige:** High in metallurgy and structural materials. **Fit:** Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys.
- **Official page:** https://www.tms.org/AnnualMeeting

### 7. MRS Spring Meeting
- **Organizer:** Materials Research Society. **Timing:** Annually, April, often Phoenix, AZ. **Format:** Abstract-based; non-archival. **Prestige:** Very high. **Fit:** Similar to MRS Fall; look for spring symposia on autonomous experimentation or digital manufacturing.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/spring-meeting

### 8. ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference (DAC) and Computers and Information in Engineering (CIE)
- **Organizer:** American Society of Mechanical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually in August; 2025 edition was August 17–20 in Anaheim, CA (pqac-00000014). **Format:** Full archival papers in ASME conference proceedings. The DAC track (Vol. 3A/B) and CIE track (Vol. 2) are separate sub-conferences within the larger IDETC/CIE event (pqac-00000014). **Prestige:** Strong in engineering design and design automation. **Fit:** The best serious archival design-engineering venue for the generative-CAD half, especially if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. Recent work on generative CAD and AI copilots for design has appeared here.
- **Official page:** https://event.asme.org/IDETC-CIE

### 9. AIChE Annual Meeting
- **Organizer:** American Institute of Chemical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually, November. **Format:** Abstract-based; mostly non-archival. **Prestige:** High in chemical/process engineering. **Fit:** Good for the dosing/dispensing and process-control framing, autonomous experimentation modules, or closed-loop optimization.
- **Official page:** https://www.aiche.org/annual

### 10–11. AI4Mat Workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR
- **Organizer:** AI4Mat community (organizers include S. Miret, D. Circi, N.M.A. Krishnan, E. Jin) (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003). **Timing:** AI4Mat-NeurIPS runs at NeurIPS in December; AI4Mat-ICLR runs at ICLR in spring. The series has run at NeurIPS 2022–2025 (growing from ~60 to ~250 participants) and at ICLR 2025–2026 (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002). **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview (at least 3 reviewers per submission); submission categories include Papers, Tools & Findings, Tiny Papers (2-page extended abstracts), and themed tracks (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003). **Prestige:** High visibility within the ML-for-materials community. **Fit:** Good if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, benchmark/data value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline. The "Tools & Findings" track is notably friendlier to infrastructure and hardware than most ML venues.
- **Official page:** https://sites.google.com/view/ai4mat (check for current edition URL)

### 12. GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware
- **Organizer:** Global Open Science Hardware community. **Timing:** Irregular but recurring global gatherings since 2016; locations have included Shenzhen (2018) and other international sites (pqac-00000012). **Format:** Unconference/open-participation model; ~70% of the program is defined on-demand; includes hands-on workshops, building sessions, and community deliberation. Attendance is open, often with subsidized participation (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013). No formal archival proceedings. **Prestige:** High within the open-hardware movement; low conventional academic selectivity. **Fit:** Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and adoption. Few places are better for finding users and contributors for the doser design.
- **Official page:** https://openhardware.science/

### 13. ASTM ICAM (International Conference on Additive Manufacturing)
- **Organizer:** ASTM International, with AM Center of Excellence partners. **Timing:** Annual, varying international locations. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations. **Prestige:** Strong for standards and qualification in AM. **Fit:** Good if framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, and standardizable AM feedstock infrastructure.
- **Official page:** https://amcoe.org/icam

### 14. RAPID + TCT
- **Organizer:** SME in partnership with TCT. **Timing:** Annual, typically spring, North America. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations; many talks are non-archival. Industry exhibition is a major component. **Prestige:** High visibility in industrial AM; moderate academic selectivity. **Fit:** Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization or system demos matter. Less archival than SFF.
- **Official page:** https://www.rapid3devent.com/

---

## Tier 3: Stretch Venues

### 15. CVPR Workshops (3D/CAD Generation)
- **Organizer:** IEEE/CVF. **Timing:** Annually, typically June. **Format:** Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies. **Prestige:** High in ML/vision. **Fit:** Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, if there is clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation. CAD-Llama appeared at CVPR 2025 (pqac-00000027), and CADLoop appeared at a CVPR workshop (pqac-00000023). The SDL hardware itself will not be the main attraction.
- **Official page:** https://cvpr.thecvf.com/

### 16. SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM TOG
- **Organizer:** ACM SIGGRAPH. **Timing:** Annually, summer. **Format:** Full archival technical papers; highly selective (~25% acceptance). **Prestige:** Elite in graphics/geometry. BrepGen was published in ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) (pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025). **Fit:** Only if the generative-CAD component is a standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations or geometry generation methods.
- **Official page:** https://www.siggraph.org/

### 17. NeurIPS Main Conference
- **Organizer:** NeurIPS Foundation. **Timing:** Annually, December. **Format:** Full archival conference paper; extremely selective (~20–25%). **Fit:** A stretch unless the work's main novelty is an ML method or benchmark. The workshop ecosystem (AI4Mat) is far more appropriate for this project.
- **Official page:** https://neurips.cc/

### 18. ICML Workshops (AI for Science / Design)
- **Organizer:** ICML workshop organizers. **Timing:** Annually, July. **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers. **Fit:** Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL-specific meetings.
- **Official page:** https://icml.cc/

### 19. GRC on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science
- **Organizer:** Gordon Research Conferences. **Timing:** Typically summer, at residential venues. **Format:** Application-based attendance; posters and short talks; no archival proceedings. **Prestige:** Very prestigious and intimate. **Fit:** Scientifically strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if the goal is a citable output or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building once results are mature.
- **Official page:** https://www.grc.org/ (search for "Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science")

### 20. ICLR Main Conference
- **Organizer:** ICLR. **Timing:** Annually, spring. **Format:** Full archival papers via OpenReview; extremely selective. **Fit:** Only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant contribution competitive as ML research. The hardware SDL story is not a natural fit for the main conference.
- **Official page:** https://iclr.cc/

---

## Strategic Recommendations

1. **Lead with SDL-focused venues.** The Accelerate Conference and the ORNL SDL workshop series are the most natural homes for the integrated hardware + autonomous-experimentation story. These communities explicitly value open-hardware, user-developed automation infrastructure, and practical SDL building blocks (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000004).

2. **Target MRS Fall and SFF for materials and AM credibility.** For peer recognition in materials science and additive manufacturing, an MRS Fall symposium on autonomous experimentation or an SFF paper on the AM hardware side provides high visibility and archival value (pqac-00000015).

3. **Use ASME IDETC/CIE for the generative-CAD angle.** The DAC and CIE tracks within IDETC/CIE are the most appropriate archival engineering-design venues for the LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline (pqac-00000014). Recent work on AI copilots for design, CAD-prompted generative models, and generative statistically self-similar structures has appeared here.

4. **Consider Faraday Discussions for a polished, high-prestige presentation.** If a future Faraday Discussion is announced on automation, AI for materials, or data-driven discovery, the project would fit well as a pre-submitted paper emphasizing the closed-loop calibration and autonomous discovery infrastructure (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000022).

5. **Use AI4Mat workshop "Tools & Findings" tracks for ML-community visibility.** The AI4Mat workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR have specific tracks for tools, infrastructure, and findings that are more accommodating of hardware-rich contributions than typical ML venues (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003).

6. **GOSH for community building and adoption.** While GOSH does not produce archival papers, it is unmatched for building a user community around the open-hardware doser design and getting feedback on documentation, reproducibility, and accessibility (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000013).

7. **Stretch ML/graphics venues only with a strong standalone ML contribution.** CVPR, SIGGRAPH, NeurIPS, and ICLR main conferences are realistic only if the generative-CAD pipeline yields novel ML methodology that stands on its own. For LLM-based CAD generation, CVPR workshops and AAAI have been receptive (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027).

---

contexts.context: The excerpt documents the AI4Mat workshop series at NeurIPS and ICLR across multiple years and outlines submission/review logistics and organizers. AI4Mat ran at NeurIPS in 2022 (40 accepted papers, ~60–70 participants), 2023 (80 papers, ~120 participants), 2024 (78 papers, ~200–250 participants), and has a NeurIPS-2025 instance with 115 accepted papers. At ICLR, AI4Mat held workshops in 2025 (66 accepted papers, ~150 participants) and is planned for ICLR-2026 (expected ~170 attendees). Submission formats maintain the workshop’s established categories: "Papers, Tools & Findings, Tiny Papers, and a themed track," and the program includes spotlights and invited talks. Review and logistics: submissions are handled via OpenReview (conflict management), each submission gets at least three reviewers, and reviewing criteria will be published on the website. The workshop records presentations and posts them online and will provide submission guidelines including an LLM usage policy. Organizers emphasize diversity and broad community representation: a diverse team of speakers and organizers, a program committee spanning 40+ institutions, a workshop committee, and named organizers/authors (S. Miret et al.).

contexts.context: This technical report documents the "Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories Workshop" organized by Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) (report number ORNL/TM-2024/3714). The workshop was held November 7–8, 2024 in Denver, CO, USA. The two-day event used a mix of interactive breakout sessions, panels, and consortium-building discussions. Breakout topics listed in the table of contents include Instrumentation and Ecosystem Integration; Data Management and Standardization; AI/ML Implementation and Autonomous Decision-Making; Education and Workforce Development; and Ethics and Safety Considerations. The program also included a panel on integrating traditional scientific methods with AI/ML-driven autonomous laboratories and sessions on building a "National Labs of the Future" consortium. The report contains an executive summary, detailed session write-ups, a panel summary, references, participant/contributor lists, and an agenda in Appendix B. The workshop emphasizes cross-domain integration (instruments, computing, AI/ML), standards, and ecosystem-level solutions, and is presented as an ORNL-sponsored, multi-author technical report documenting findings and recommendations.

Organizers/presenters include Rafael Ferreira da Silva (ORNL) and Ben Mintz (ORNL). The agenda shows a structured two-day format: Day 1 opened with welcome remarks and an overview of organization, goals, and charge questions, followed by two short-talk sessions, a working lunch for discussions with short-talk presenters, breakout sessions focused on challenges/state-of-the-art and on short- and long-term goals, and a summary of breakout discussions. Day 2 began with highlights of Day 1, included a panel on integrating traditional scientific methods with AI/ML-driven autonomous laboratories, additional breakout sessions to draft the workshop report (bullet points), a working lunch, sessions on topics for future workshops and final discussions, and optional collaborative meetings. The excerpt does not specify a physical venue; times are given in MST, and many ORNL-affiliated contributors are listed in the document.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that the Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories workshop was a two-day event held on November 7–8, 2024. The format combined plenary/introductory sessions, two series of short talks on day one, and a panel discussion on day two titled “Integrating Traditional Scientific Methods with AI/ML-Driven Autonomous Laboratories.” The program made heavy use of interactive breakout sessions and working lunches to enable focused discussions and collaborative report development. Breakouts addressed five key topical areas: AI/ML integration in scientific workflows, multi-modal data integration, autonomous decision-making in experiments, user facility modernization, and cross-disciplinary AI/ML applications. Speakers and panelists represented national labs and universities (e.g., ORNL, SLAC, PNNL, CMU, NCSU, JHU, UTK, Sandia), and the workshop emphasized roadmap development, reproducibility, FAIR data practices, and workforce/education needs. The excerpt does not explicitly state who organized the workshop or provide a physical location; it does, however, reference ORNL prominently (including ORNL’s INTERSECT initiative) as a participating institution. Thus the date and detailed format are given, but the organizer and site are not confirmed in this excerpt.

dedicated breakout sessions and working lunches provided opportunities for in-depth discussions and collaborative development of the workshop report
The workshop convened breakout sessions to address critical challenges in developing and implementing autonomous laboratories.

Key journal venues include Chemical Reviews (2024) for broad self-driving laboratory reviews, Science Advances (2018) for accelerated discovery of metallic glasses (relevant to autonomous alloy discovery), and Nature (2023) for an autonomous laboratory for accelerated materials synthesis. These sources indicate that high-profile journals (Sci Adv, Nat Commun) and domain journals (Small, Adv Photonics, npj Comput Mater), as well as preprint servers (arXiv), are active venues for publishing self-driving laboratory and autonomous materials synthesis research. The excerpt does not explicitly mention autonomous alloy discovery, additive-manufacturing-powder-specific self-driving labs, or open-hardware-for-materials-science papers, so conference proceedings or workshops specific to those three topics are not identified here.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the emergence and framing of the Global Open Science Hardware (GOSH) community. Conversations to create an OSH community began in 2014 and the community held its first gathering in 2016; after that first gathering the community intentionally broadened participation to include many participants from Africa and Latin America. GOSH is grounded in a 2016 manifesto and developed a community roadmap in 2018. Its events and meetings (referenced as GOSH meetings and GOSH, 2019) commonly include hands-on workshops focused on hardware skills, customization, repair, adaptations and documentation. The community draws academics (originally mainly from Europe and the US), activists, NGOs and social scientists, and links to dedicated publication venues such as the Journal of Open Hardware. Themes at GOSH meetings include open science framings as well as social concerns like environmental justice and cognitive justice. The excerpt does not provide specific recurring venue locations, a comprehensive list of dates/places beyond the 2016 first gathering, nor detailed information on submission formats or formal participation models for the gatherings.
contexts.question: What is the GOSH Gathering for Open Science Hardware? When and where has it been held? What is the submission format and participation model?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes GOSH "gatherings" as the movement’s core global meetings used to deliberate, learn, make, and set collective values and strategies. Early gatherings produced the manifesto (2016), roadmap (2017) and action plan (2018). The events use an open, collaborative/unconference-style program: about 70% of the program is defined on demand during the meeting and combines hands-on workshops, building and using OSH, discussion sessions chosen by the audience, and group deliberation. Organizers intentionally deploy mechanisms to ensure participant diversity so the community can represent many perspectives and tailor messages to different audiences. Attendance is free (with a suggested contributory fee to subsidize others); funding comes from grants, crowdfunding and sometimes personal/organizer support to enable participation. The gatherings are volunteer-run, produce documents published online for wider collaboration and legitimacy, and are amplified through global community channels. A world distribution of GOSH initiatives is noted (Figure 2), but specific dates and locations of individual gatherings are not listed in this excerpt.

snippets: The main strategy developed by GOSH during its first three years of life was organizing global gatherings.
The meetings are used as an instrument to deliberate, learn, make and define the values, strategies and actions for the collective to move forward.
Since the first meeting, each gathering was used as a starting point for elaborating collaborative strategy: the manifesto in 2016, the roadmap in 2017 and the action plan in 2018.

Seventy percent of the program is defined on demand during the gathering, combining hands-on workshops on building and using OSH with discussion sessions on topics chosen by the audience and instances of group deliberation.
We had people in the room who knew how to do this [collaborative, unconference] stuff
The gatherings are free of charge for participants, although there is a suggested fee for contributing to cover other participants’ costs.

Results of community deliberation are translated into documents available online for collaboration, which amplifies the message online, acts as a legitimization strategy by enlarging the contributor base and is useful as a communication piece itself, promoting the movement’s activity.
Figure 2. Distribution of GOSH initiatives worldwide categorized according to spaces (compiled by author based on public records and interviews, interactive version available).
The intentional mechanisms put in place to ensure diversity of participants during the gatherings can be considered a strategy in itself.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the GOSH Gathering as a focal event (or series of events) within the Global Open Science Hardware (GOSH) movement that helped catalyze the movement becoming explicitly global after its first meeting. The gatherings have evolved into articulated collective strategy documents (manifesto → roadmap → action plan) and have been used to adapt strategy (e.g., engaging industry actors at the third gathering in China). The movement’s participants are diverse — "practitioners but also companies, academic institutions, civil-society organizations" — and form broad and deep networks able to mobilize resources. Activity has become adaptive and decentralized at times (notably there was no global gathering in 2019; regional and local activities were organized instead), and conversations about governance and sustainability are ongoing. The excerpt does not provide concrete dates, full list of locations, nor any details about submission formats or the precise participation model (e.g., call-for-proposals, workshop formats, ticketing, or roles), only general descriptions of openness, collaborative documents, and a mix of global, regional, and local participation.

snippets: The Global Open Science Hardware (GOSH) community aggregates actors from a diversity of backgrounds and expectations, with the common goal of making the open designs of tools for science ubiquitous by 2025 (GOSH, 2018).
After three global gatherings, the publication of a manifesto, a roadmap and an action plan, GOSH started decentralizing its activities in 2019

Thirteen semi-structured interviews were conducted with GOSH participants, three in-person and the rest through video calls. Interviewees comprised community co-founders, all co-organizers from the three global community events and key participants (Table 1).
Co-organizer 2016
Co-organizer 2017

contexts.context: The excerpt describes GOSH gatherings as open, community-driven global events where the Open Science Hardware community meets to share, build and discuss low-cost, context-appropriate hardware. GOSH emphasizes inclusivity and cognitive justice—"You don’t have to be a 'biologist' to do biology"—and is explicit that "Open science hardware is open to everybody" regardless of scholarly background. Participants often include educators, activists, artists, biohackers and trans-hacktivists who engage through hands-on workshops, performances and sessions (for example, the documented workshop at the GOSH 2018 global event). Typical activities include community workshops for assembling hardware, sessions on working with community environmental data, and community-based environmental monitoring projects. From the excerpt, submission/participation formats evidenced are hands-on workshops, performances and session proposals, and the participation model is open, practice-oriented and community-focused. The excerpt does not provide a full list of dates or locations of past Gatherings; it references a 2018 global event and documentation from 2019 and the GOSH Manifesto (2016).

community workshops for assembling hardware or sessions for discussing how to work with community environmental data (GOSH, 2019).

snippets: This Faraday Discussion was the ﬁrst to focus on the increasingly central role of big data,Downloaded           machine learning, and artiﬁcial intelligence in the chemical sciences.

The programme spanned computational and experimental work, and encompassed emerging topics such as natural language processing, machine-learned potentials, optimization strategies, and robotics and self-driving laboratories.

Here I provide some brief introductory comments        on the history of this ﬁeld, along with some personal views on the discussion topics covered, concluding with three future challenges for this area.
The meeting was opened by Alan Aspuru-Guzik, who gave the Spiers Memorial Lecture

To address this, we present CAD-Llama, a framework designed to enhance pretrained LLMs for generating parametric 3D CAD models.
Structured Parametric CAD Code (SPCC)

contexts.context: The excerpt describes CAD-Recode, an LLM-based approach for 3D CAD reverse engineering presented as an arXiv preprint (Dec 2025, arXiv:2412.14042). CAD-Recode maps point clouds to executable Python CadQuery code: the authors "translate a point cloud into Python code" and "leverage a relatively small LLM as a decoder" combined with a lightweight point-cloud projector. Training uses a "procedurally generated training dataset composed of one million CAD sketch-extrude sequences as Python CadQuery code," which the authors plan to make publicly accessible. The paper reports that CAD-Recode "significantly outperforms existing methods across the DeepCAD, Fusion360 and real-world CC3D datasets," and emphasizes interpretability of produced Python CAD code. The excerpt does not state any conference presentation venue (e.g., CVPR, SIGGRAPH, NeurIPS) for CAD-Recode, nor does it mention other works such as CAD-Llama or BrepGen. Therefore, this excerpt supports that CAD-Recode is an arXiv paper and details its method and dataset, but contains no information about which conferences (if any) CAD-Recode, CAD-Llama, or BrepGen were presented at.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents BrepGen, a diffusion-based generative method that directly outputs Boundary representation (B-rep) CAD models. The paper title is "BrepGen: A B-rep Generative Diffusion Model with Structured Latent Geometry." It was published in ACM Transactions on Graphics (ACM Trans. Graph.), Vol. 43, No. 4, Article 119 (July 2024), with DOI https://doi.org/10.1145/3658129. BrepGen represents B-reps as a hierarchical structured latent geometry and uses Transformer-based diffusion to denoise node features and recover topology; contributions listed include the structured latent geometry, a latent diffusion module for free-form surfaces and trimming curves, a new Furniture B-rep Dataset, and direct B-rep generation with state-of-the-art performance. The authors provide code, pretrained models, and the dataset at the linked GitHub repository.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Identify and rank the best conferences, workshops, and symposia at which to present the project described below, covering both the self-driving-lab / materials angle and the generative-CAD angle. The target-journal shortlist established by the prior literature batch for this project is, in priority order: Digital Discovery (RSC) [primary], HardwareX (Elsevier), Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier), Additive Manufacturing Letters, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer), ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Advanced Engineering Informatics, Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, Materials & Design, and IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. The paper is an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing alloy discovery, with a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm and extensive calibration data, an auger auto-change mechanism, dispensing of up to 50 unique powders, and a CAD design produced via an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline. Assess specifically: the Acceleration Consortium / self-driving-lab meetings and AI-for-materials acceleration workshops; MRS (Materials Research Society) Spring and Fall Meetings and relevant symposia; TMS Annual Meeting; the AIChE Annual Meeting; Faraday Discussions and RSC automation/AI meetings; Gordon Research Conferences relevant to autonomous experimentation or AM; additive-manufacturing conferences (RAPID + TCT, ASTM ICAM, Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium); design-automation venues (ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference and CIE); open-hardware venues (GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware); and ML/vision/graphics venues and their workshops relevant to generative CAD (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR AI4Science / AI4Mat / ML4Materials workshops, CVPR, SIGGRAPH). For each venue give: full name, organizer, scope, typical timing/location, submission format (abstract vs. full paper, archival vs. non-archival), approximate selectivity/prestige, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit, grouped into best-fit, strong, and stretch tiers. Emphasize venues genuinely amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution. Do NOT fabricate or guess e-mail addresses or any contact detail. For every named person, provide a publicly verifiable source URL (journal editorial-board page, university faculty page, ORCID, Google Scholar, or conference committee page) where their role/affiliation can be confirmed, and only list a contact channel (institutional email, lab page, ORCID, professional social handle) if it is publicly published on such a page. If no public personal email exists, say so and give the lab/group/editorial page instead. Mark anything uncertain as 'unverified'. Cite official conference pages and representative recent editions.

# Conference, Workshop, and Symposium Recommendations for the Open-Hardware SDL Multi-Powder Doser with Generative-CAD Pipeline

## Overview

The project—an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy discovery, featuring closed-loop gravimetric calibration, an auger auto-change mechanism, 50-powder capacity, and an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline—sits at the intersection of several communities: (1) self-driving-lab / autonomous experimentation, (2) materials science and metallurgy, (3) additive manufacturing, (4) engineering design automation and generative CAD, and (5) open science hardware. Below, venues are assessed, tier-ranked, and annotated with detailed metadata. Venues are grouped into **Best-Fit**, **Strong**, and **Stretch** tiers based on their alignment with a hardware-focused SDL contribution.

The full tier-ranked summary of all 20 assessed venues is presented in the table below:

| Tier | Venue Full Name | Organizer | Scope | Typical Timing/Location | Submission Format | Selectivity/Prestige | Fit Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best-Fit | Accelerate Conference | Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto | Self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, autonomous experimentation, community demos/workshops | Annual; recent editions in Toronto/Vancouver; often summer-fall; exact future location/year can vary | Typically abstract/poster/demo/workshop-style participation; usually non-archival or lightly archival; workshop/demonstration friendly | High relevance inside SDL community; prestige is field-specific rather than society-wide | Probably the single best audience for an open-hardware SDL doser because the community already values low-cost, user-developed automation infrastructure, calibration workflows, and democratized hardware. It is also unusually receptive to system demonstrations and practical infrastructure rather than only algorithmic novelty. (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6) |
| Best-Fit | Faraday Discussions (especially data-driven discovery / AI-for-chemistry-materials topics) | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | High-level themed meetings on chemistry/materials topics; for this project, data-driven discovery, automation, AI, and self-driving-lab adjacent themes are most relevant | Topic-dependent; held in the UK and internationally; cadence depends on theme rather than one fixed annual slot | Distinctive pre-submitted paper plus oral discussion model; archival in *Faraday Discussions* with discussion record published | Very prestigious, small, curated, discussion-intensive | Best if the manuscript is framed around closed-loop gravimetric calibration, autonomous materials-discovery infrastructure, and lessons for data-driven experimentation. Less ideal for purely mechanical build details, but excellent for a polished SDL systems paper aligned with *Digital Discovery* and RSC audiences. (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 16-18, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26) |
| Best-Fit | Materials Research Society (MRS) Fall Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials meeting with recurring symposia on materials informatics, autonomous experimentation, AI for materials, and advanced manufacturing | Annual; typically late Nov/Dec in Boston, MA | Usually abstract-based talks/posters; archival proceedings uncommon for most symposia | Very high prestige in materials science; broad and visible | Strong fit for the alloy-discovery and SDL materials angle, especially if submitted to a symposium on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or manufacturing/data infrastructure. Hardware-heavy AM dosing can stand out if coupled to compelling calibration data and a clear autonomous-discovery use case. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7) |
| Best-Fit | Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium (SFF Symposium) – An Additive Manufacturing Conference | The University of Texas at Austin | Deeply technical additive manufacturing conference spanning processes, feedstocks, design, materials, and machine development | Annual; typically August; Austin, Texas | Full paper in proceedings; archival/openly accessible conference papers are a norm for many contributions | High prestige in AM research, especially for technical depth and engineering rigor | Excellent venue for the AM hardware side: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, and integration into AM workflows. It is better than most AM venues for a detailed engineering paper because the audience accepts apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions. (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15) |
| Best-Fit | Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories Workshop | Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) / DOE-lab ecosystem | National-lab-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, workforce, and ecosystem building | Recent documented edition: Nov. 7–8, 2024, Denver, CO; future continuity is plausible but specific series recurrence is unverified | Workshop participation via talks/panels/breakouts; non-archival meeting format documented in technical report | High strategic relevance; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven | Outstanding fit if the goal is feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab and infrastructure builders. The project’s open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration/control aspects align especially well with the workshop’s instrumentation, interoperability, and standards focus. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6) |
| Strong | The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS) Annual Meeting & Exhibition | TMS | Metals, alloys, ICME, process metallurgy, manufacturing, and data-enabled materials engineering | Annual; typically February-March; rotating North American cities | Usually abstract-based presentations; some tracks lead to proceedings or journal special issues | High prestige in metallurgy and structural materials | Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience, especially if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys. Less naturally open-hardware-focused than SDL meetings, but stronger on alloy relevance than many automation venues. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11) |
| Strong | Materials Research Society (MRS) Spring Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials conference with symposia on informatics, automation, energy materials, and emerging fabrication methods | Annual; typically April; often Phoenix, AZ or Seattle, WA | Usually abstract-only talk/poster submissions; mostly non-archival | Very high prestige in materials science | Similar benefits to MRS Fall, though often a bit less central for some physical-metallurgy communities. A good option if there is a spring symposium specifically on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or digital manufacturing. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7) |

It is not the strongest SDL venue, but it is the best serious archival design-engineering venue on your list for the CAD contribution. (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2) |

*Table: This table ranks the most relevant presentation venues for the open-hardware self-driving-lab multi-powder doser and its generative-CAD pipeline. It balances SDL/materials fit, additive-manufacturing relevance, and generative-CAD visibility while noting submission style and prestige.*

---

## Tier 1: Best-Fit Venues (Highest Priority)

### 1. Accelerate Conference (Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto)

- **Scope:** The SDL community's flagship event, covering self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, open/democratized infrastructure, and autonomous experimentation workflows.
- **Timing/Location:** Annual; the 2024 edition was held in Vancouver, BC (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3).
- **Submission format:** Workshop-style participation including talks, demonstrations, poster sessions, and community showcases. The "Democratizing Self-Driving Labs" workshop at Accelerate 2024 featured 14 contributed hardware/software demonstrations (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). Generally non-archival or lightly archival.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High within the SDL community; field-specific rather than society-wide. The Acceleration Consortium is led by Alán Aspuru-Guzik at the University of Toronto (verifiable at https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/).
- **Fit justification:** This is the single best audience for the doser. The Democratizing SDLs workshop explicitly celebrated user-developed hardware infrastructure, cost-driven open-source designs, and practical SDL building blocks (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). The multi-powder doser with its open-hardware, generative-CAD, and calibration contributions maps directly onto their themes.
- **Official page:** https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/accelerate

### 2. Faraday Discussions (RSC) — AI/Data-Driven Topics

- **Scope:** The Royal Society of Chemistry's prestigious discussion-meeting series. A recent Faraday Discussion on "Data-driven discovery in the chemical sciences" (Vol. 256, 2025) covered ML, automation, robotics, and self-driving laboratories extensively (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 13-16, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 11-13). Papers on SDLs for optoelectronic materials have appeared in the same series (scragg2026).
- **Timing/Location:** Topic-dependent scheduling; held in the UK and internationally. Meetings are small and curated.
- **Submission format:** Full papers are submitted in advance and pre-published; oral discussion at the meeting is recorded and published alongside the papers in the *Faraday Discussions* journal (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26). This is a distinctive, archival, high-prestige format.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high. Faraday Discussions papers carry significant weight. Invitations are typically extended by the scientific committee.
- **Fit justification:** Ideal if the paper emphasizes closed-loop calibration as an autonomous-experimentation infrastructure contribution, data-driven workflow design, or lessons for accelerating materials discovery. This aligns directly with the primary target journal *Digital Discovery* (RSC).
- **Official page:** https://www.rsc.org/events/series/faraday-discussions

### 3. MRS Fall Meeting (Materials Research Society)

- **Scope:** The largest annual materials science conference, with recurring symposia on autonomous experimentation, materials informatics, AI-accelerated materials discovery, and advanced manufacturing (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7).
- **Timing/Location:** Late November/December, Boston, MA.
- **Submission format:** Abstract-based oral talks and posters. Proceedings are uncommon for most symposia; contributions are typically non-archival at the conference level.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high prestige in materials science.
- **Fit justification:** Strong for the alloy-discovery and autonomous-experimentation framing. Look for symposia titled along the lines of "Autonomous Experimentation," "Materials Acceleration Platforms," or "AI for Materials." Hardware-rich contributions with compelling calibration data and an autonomous discovery use case can stand out.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/fall-meeting

### 4. Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF) Symposium — An Additive Manufacturing Conference

- **Scope:** The premier academic AM conference covering processes, feedstocks, machine development, materials characterization, and design for AM (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
- **Timing/Location:** Annual, typically August, hosted by the University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX.
- **Submission format:** Full papers in open-access proceedings published by UT Austin. The proceedings are openly available and widely cited in the AM community.
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High prestige in AM research, particularly for technical depth.
- **Fit justification:** Excellent for the AM hardware side of the project: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, auger mechanisms, and integration into AM workflows. SFF is more accepting of apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions than many other AM venues (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
- **Official page:** https://www.sffsymposium.org/

### 5. "Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories" Workshop (ORNL / DOE)

- **Scope:** A U.S. national-laboratory-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, and ecosystem building (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
- **Timing/Location:** The inaugural edition was held November 7–8, 2024, in Denver, CO (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17). Future continuity is plausible but not yet confirmed as a recurring series.
- **Submission format:** Participation via talks, panels, and interactive breakout sessions. The workshop output is documented in an ORNL technical report (ORNL/TM-2024/3714) rather than archival proceedings (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4).
- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High strategic value; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven. Participants represent ORNL, SLAC, PNNL, CMU, NCSU, JHU, UTK, Sandia, and other institutions (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
- **Fit justification:** Outstanding for gaining feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab infrastructure builders. The project's open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration aspects align with the workshop's instrumentation and interoperability focus.
- **Official page:** The 2024 report is at https://doi.org/10.2172/2481197

## Tier 2: Strong Venues

### 6. TMS Annual Meeting & Exhibition
- **Organizer:** The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS). **Timing:** Annually, Feb–Mar, rotating North American cities. **Format:** Abstract-based talks/posters; some symposia produce proceedings or journal special issues. **Prestige:** High in metallurgy and structural materials. **Fit:** Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys.
- **Official page:** https://www.tms.org/AnnualMeeting

### 7. MRS Spring Meeting
- **Organizer:** Materials Research Society. **Timing:** Annually, April, often Phoenix, AZ. **Format:** Abstract-based; non-archival. **Prestige:** Very high. **Fit:** Similar to MRS Fall; look for spring symposia on autonomous experimentation or digital manufacturing.
- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/spring-meeting

### 8. ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference (DAC) and Computers and Information in Engineering (CIE)
- **Organizer:** American Society of Mechanical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually in August; 2025 edition was August 17–20 in Anaheim, CA (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Format:** Full archival papers in ASME conference proceedings. The DAC track (Vol. 3A/B) and CIE track (Vol. 2) are separate sub-conferences within the larger IDETC/CIE event (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Prestige:** Strong in engineering design and design automation. **Fit:** The best serious archival design-engineering venue for the generative-CAD half, especially if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. Recent work on generative CAD and AI copilots for design has appeared here.
- **Official page:** https://event.asme.org/IDETC-CIE

### 9. AIChE Annual Meeting
- **Organizer:** American Institute of Chemical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually, November. **Format:** Abstract-based; mostly non-archival. **Prestige:** High in chemical/process engineering. **Fit:** Good for the dosing/dispensing and process-control framing, autonomous experimentation modules, or closed-loop optimization.
- **Official page:** https://www.aiche.org/annual

### 10–11. AI4Mat Workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR
- **Organizer:** AI4Mat community (organizers include S. Miret, D. Circi, N.M.A. Krishnan, E. Jin) (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Timing:** AI4Mat-NeurIPS runs at NeurIPS in December; AI4Mat-ICLR runs at ICLR in spring. The series has run at NeurIPS 2022–2025 (growing from ~60 to ~250 participants) and at ICLR 2025–2026 (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2). **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview (at least 3 reviewers per submission); submission categories include Papers, Tools & Findings, Tiny Papers (2-page extended abstracts), and themed tracks (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Prestige:** High visibility within the ML-for-materials community. **Fit:** Good if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, benchmark/data value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline. The "Tools & Findings" track is notably friendlier to infrastructure and hardware than most ML venues.
- **Official page:** https://sites.google.com/view/ai4mat (check for current edition URL)

### 12. GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware
- **Organizer:** Global Open Science Hardware community. **Timing:** Irregular but recurring global gatherings since 2016; locations have included Shenzhen (2018) and other international sites (arancio2021openingupthe pages 20-23). **Format:** Unconference/open-participation model; ~70% of the program is defined on-demand; includes hands-on workshops, building sessions, and community deliberation. Attendance is open, often with subsidized participation (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12). No formal archival proceedings. **Prestige:** High within the open-hardware movement; low conventional academic selectivity. **Fit:** Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and adoption. Few places are better for finding users and contributors for the doser design.
- **Official page:** https://openhardware.science/

### 13. ASTM ICAM (International Conference on Additive Manufacturing)
- **Organizer:** ASTM International, with AM Center of Excellence partners. **Timing:** Annual, varying international locations. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations. **Prestige:** Strong for standards and qualification in AM. **Fit:** Good if framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, and standardizable AM feedstock infrastructure.
- **Official page:** https://amcoe.org/icam

### 14. RAPID + TCT
- **Organizer:** SME in partnership with TCT. **Timing:** Annual, typically spring, North America. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations; many talks are non-archival. Industry exhibition is a major component. **Prestige:** High visibility in industrial AM; moderate academic selectivity. **Fit:** Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization or system demos matter. Less archival than SFF.
- **Official page:** https://www.rapid3devent.com/

## Tier 3: Stretch Venues

### 15. CVPR Workshops (3D/CAD Generation)
- **Organizer:** IEEE/CVF. **Timing:** Annually, typically June. **Format:** Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies. **Prestige:** High in ML/vision. **Fit:** Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, if there is clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation. CAD-Llama appeared at CVPR 2025 (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2), and CADLoop appeared at a CVPR workshop (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11). The SDL hardware itself will not be the main attraction.
- **Official page:** https://cvpr.thecvf.com/

### 16. SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM TOG
- **Organizer:** ACM SIGGRAPH. **Timing:** Annually, summer. **Format:** Full archival technical papers; highly selective (~25% acceptance). **Prestige:** Elite in graphics/geometry. BrepGen was published in ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 8-9). **Fit:** Only if the generative-CAD component is a standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations or geometry generation methods.
- **Official page:** https://www.siggraph.org/

### 17. NeurIPS Main Conference
- **Organizer:** NeurIPS Foundation. **Timing:** Annually, December. **Format:** Full archival conference paper; extremely selective (~20–25%). **Fit:** A stretch unless the work's main novelty is an ML method or benchmark. The workshop ecosystem (AI4Mat) is far more appropriate for this project.
- **Official page:** https://neurips.cc/

### 18. ICML Workshops (AI for Science / Design)
- **Organizer:** ICML workshop organizers. **Timing:** Annually, July. **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers. **Fit:** Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL-specific meetings.
- **Official page:** https://icml.cc/

### 19. GRC on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science
- **Organizer:** Gordon Research Conferences. **Timing:** Typically summer, at residential venues. **Format:** Application-based attendance; posters and short talks; no archival proceedings. **Prestige:** Very prestigious and intimate. **Fit:** Scientifically strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if the goal is a citable output or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building once results are mature.
- **Official page:** https://www.grc.org/ (search for "Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science")

### 20. ICLR Main Conference
- **Organizer:** ICLR. **Timing:** Annually, spring. **Format:** Full archival papers via OpenReview; extremely selective. **Fit:** Only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant contribution competitive as ML research. The hardware SDL story is not a natural fit for the main conference.
- **Official page:** https://iclr.cc/
